questions.question: What is the biggest weakness of taking system design notes as a full transcript?
options: They are harder to skim later for assumptions, numbers, and trade-offs that actually matter.
They cannot be stored in markdown files.
They prevent the use of architecture diagrams entirely.
They make it impossible to discuss failure modes.
questions.explanation: Transcript-style notes preserve chronology, but not reusable reasoning.

questions.question: Which section should appear before architecture in good design notes?
options: A long glossary of distributed systems terms.
A capacity model with assumptions and rough sizing.
An appendix of rejected databases.
A transcript of interviewer interruptions.
questions.explanation: Sizing should anchor later design decisions.

questions.question: Why is a fixed note template valuable across many lessons?
options: It makes later comparison and retrieval faster, even when the prompts differ.
It guarantees that every design answer is correct.
It removes the need for trade-off discussion.
It allows the same architecture diagram to be reused unchanged.
questions.explanation: Stable structure reduces cognitive load and exposes recurring gaps.

questions.question: Which note section most directly prevents polished but shallow write-ups?
options: A dedicated trade-off and what-I-missed section.
An emoji legend for urgency.
A count of how many services appeared in the diagram.
A list of every technology the candidate has used before.
questions.explanation: Explicitly recording trade-offs and misses preserves reasoning quality rather than only outcomes.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping notes short and bullet-heavy?
options: They are faster to review, but nuanced reasoning may need careful rewriting to avoid ambiguity.
They increase storage cost substantially over narrative notes.
They make diagrams impossible to include.
They remove the need for a debrief section.
questions.explanation: Concise notes are useful, but they still need enough specificity to remain interpretable.

questions.question: Which signal best shows that a note-taking system is healthy?
options: Most notes contain explicit assumptions, sizing, and at least one trade-off that can be skimmed quickly.
The notes use the longest possible markdown documents.
Every note includes a complete production rollout plan.
The learner rewrites old notes from scratch every week.
questions.explanation: The healthy signal is reusable reasoning density, not document length.

questions.question: If a mock partner needs to pick up your old design note cold, what matters most?
options: The note should reveal scope, assumptions, key numbers, and why major decisions were made.
The note should preserve every sentence spoken during the session.
The note should rank database products by popularity.
The note should omit unresolved questions to appear polished.
questions.explanation: Portable notes need enough context for another person to continue the reasoning.

questions.question: Why is a separate debrief block useful in the same note template?
options: It keeps hindsight, mistakes, and next actions distinct from the original design reasoning.
It replaces the need for a progress tracker.
It guarantees the next prompt will be easier.
It allows architecture sections to ignore failure modes.
questions.explanation: Separating debrief content preserves both the answer and the learning loop.
